records.ety: [F., fr. L. secundus second, properly, following, fr. sequi to follow. See Sue to follow, and cf. Secund.]

defs: Immediately following the first; next to the first in order of place or time; hence, occurring again; another; other.
quotes: And he slept and dreamed the second time.

defs: Next to the first in value, power, excellence, dignity, or rank; secondary; subordinate; inferior.
quotes: May the day when we become the second people upon earth . . . be the day of our utter extirpation.

defs: Being of the same kind as another that has preceded; another, like a prototype; as, a second Cato; a second Troy; a second deluge.
quotes: A Daniel, still say I, a second Daniel!
On second thoughts, gentlemen, I don't wish you had known him.

defs: One who, or that which, follows, or comes after; one next and inferior in place, time, rank, importance, excellence, or power.
quotes: Man An angel's second, nor his second long.

defs: One who follows or attends another for his support and aid; a backer; an assistant; specifically, one who acts as another's aid in a duel.
quotes: Being sure enough of seconds after the first onset.
Give second, and my love Is everlasting thine.

defs: An article of merchandise of a grade inferior to the best; esp., a coarse or inferior kind of flour.

The sixtieth part of a minute of time or of a minute of space, that is, the second regular subdivision of the degree; as, sound moves about 1,140 English feet in a second; five minutes and ten seconds north of this place.
In the duodecimal system of mensuration, the twelfth part of an inch or prime; a line. See Inch, and Prime, n., 8.
The interval between any tone and the tone which is represented on the degree of the staff next above it.
The second part in a concerted piece; -- often popularly applied to the alto.
A motion in support of another motion which has been moved in a deliberative body; a motion without a second dies without discussion.

records.ety: [Cf. F. seconder, L. secundare, from secundus. See Second, a.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Seconded; p. pr. & vb. n. Seconding.]

defs: To follow in the next place; to succeed; to alternate.
quotes: In the method of nature, a low valley is immediately seconded with an ambitious hill.

defs: To follow or attend for the purpose of assisting; to support; to back; to act as the second of; to assist; to forward; to encourage.
quotes: We have supplies to second our attempt.
In human works though labored on with pain, A thousand movements scarce one purpose gain; In God's, one single can its end produce, Yet serves to second too some other use.

defs: to support, as a motion{6} or proposal, by adding one's voice to that of the mover or proposer.
notes: ☞ Under common parliamentary rules used by many organizations, especially legislative bodies, a motion must be seconded in order to come properly before the deliberative body for discussion. Any motion{6} for which there is no second{8} dies for lack thereof.
